translations.source: Records of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]Scroll 39 (Shu 9)
translations.source_text:
若無興德之言，則戮允等以彰其慢

願陛下託臣以討賊興復之效；不效，則治臣之罪，以告先帝之靈；責攸之、禕、允等之慢，以彰其咎
translations.translation:
Dong Yun (Xiuzhao)
董允 (休昭)
Lived AD ?–246
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 39 (Shu 9)
byZL181
onMarch 1st, 2025

AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'created_at' changed to '1714018204' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
Dong Yun, styledXiuzhao, was the son ofHousehold General who Controls the Army,[Dong] He.
When theFormer Lordinstalled theHeir Apparent,[Dong] Yunwas chosen to be aStaff Member, then shifted toHorse Washer. After theLatter Lordsucceeded the throne, he was promoted toAttending Gentleman at the Yellow Gates. WhenAssisting Chancellor[Zhuge] Liangwas going on an expedition to the north, he stationed atHanzhong[漢中]. He worried that as theLatter Lordwas callow in age, he would find it troublesome to distinguish the virtuous and the evil. Because[Dong] Yunhad a heart that was just and loyal, he wanted to have him handle palace affairs. Thus, he petitioned:
“Palace AttendantsGuo YouzhiandFei YiandAttending GentlemanDong Yun,1among others, have been picked by theformer emperorto entrust them withYour Majesty. Giving deep consideration over gains and losses, and wholeheartedly proposing loyal words is their responsibility. I believe that regarding the affairs within the palace, consult them about everything, regardless of how major or minor the affairs are; they would always be able to fix any oversights and make enhancements. If they do not have any words that promote virtue, then kill[Dong] Yunand the others to reveal their tardiness.2”•••
The full petition in thebiography ofZhuge Lianggroups all of these three men underPalace AttendantandAttending Gentlemanunlike this biography, which groupsGuo YouzhiandFei YiasPalace AttendantandDong YunasAttending Gentleman.↩
“If they do not have any words that promote virtue, then kill[Dong] Yunand the others to reveal their tardiness” [若無興德之言，則戮允等以彰其慢] is not in the full petition in thebiography ofZhuge Liang. Instead, there is a similar but different sentence: “I hopeYour Majestywill entrust me with the efficiency of suppressing the traitors and bringing about a resurgence. If I am ineffective, then punish me for my crimes, inform the spirit of the departed emperor, and blame the tardiness of[Guo] Youzhi,[Fei] Yi,[Dong] Yun, and others, so as to reveal their mistakes.” [願陛下託臣以討賊興復之效；不效，則治臣之罪，以告先帝之靈；責攸之、禕、允等之慢，以彰其咎]. The reason for this difference is not known.↩
[Zhuge] Liangsoon invited[Fei] Yito beMilitary Adjutant.[Dong] Yunwas promoted toPalace AttendantandHousehold General of the Rapid Tigers, and he was given control of the nocturnal guards3and [imperial] bodyguards.[Guo] Youzhiwas always harmonious and compliant by nature, but he filled offices, nothingmore.A•••
“Nocturnal guards” [宿衞] are soldiers that guarded the palace.↩
TheEminent Worthies of Chu Statestates:[Guo] Youzhiwas fromNanyang[南陽]. Due to his talent and deeds, he gained fame in his time.
The responsibilities for presenting [opinions] for adoption were all undertaken by[Dong] Yun. When[Dong] Yunwas managing matters, he established regulations for preemptive purposes.4He did his best according to the logic of rectifying andsalvaging.5•••
These stringent regulations were meant to prevent abuse of power.↩
Dong Yundid his best to correct bad behavior and rescued people from a wicked path.↩
TheLatter Lordoften wanted to select [women] to fill the harem, but[Dong] Yunbelieved that as the number of empresses and consorts for the Son of Heaven did not exceed twenty in antiquity, and as the concubines were already filled, it would be inappropriate to increase it. He remained persistent and unyielding to the end. TheLatter Lordincreasingly felt respect and fear for him.
When theDirector of the Secretariat,Jiang Wan, was serving asInspectorofYi province[益州刺史], he memorialized to give titles toFei Yiand[Dong] Yun. He petitioned, “[Dong] Yunhas served in the government for several years, assisting the imperial house. He should be conferred peerage and land to laud his accomplishments.”[Dong] Yunfirmly declined and did not accept.
When theLatter Lordwas an adult, he doted on the eunuchHuang Hao.[Huang] Haowas sycophantic and had guile, and he wanted to be accepted for entry.6[Dong] Yunoften had a righteous expression when correcting hissovereign, in a higher sense, and made several rebukes to[Huang] Hao, in a lower sense.[Huang] Haodreaded[Dong] Yunand did not dare act wrongly. Until the end of the time of[Dong] Yun,[Huang] Haodid not surpass the rank ofDeputy of the Yellow Gates.•••
This is basically stating thatHuang Haowanted to climb up the hierachy.↩
[Dong] Yunhad once set a date to travel and feast jointly with theDirector of the Secretariat,Fei Yi, and theArmy Controller of the Center,Hu Ji, among others. The preparations for the items and carriages was already done whenPalace GentlemanDong HuiofXiangyang[襄陽] went to[Dong] Yunto pay his respects. This[Dong] Huiwas young in age and low in office. When he saw[Dong] Yunparked to depart, he hesitated and asked to leave.[Dong] Yunrefused, saying, “The original reason I was leaving was because I wanted to roam and chat with my friends. Yet now that you, sir, have deigned yourself [to come here], this is when we should reveal our accrued feelings7to each other. For me to leave this conversation to go to some other banquet is unbecoming.” Thus, he ordered the carriage horses to be disbanded.[Fei] Yiand the others stopped their chariots and did not move. His adherence to propriety and deigning to scholars were all of thissort.B•••
Here, “accrued feelings” [闊積] refers to the feeling of missing each other after a long period of time.↩
TheAnnals of Xiangyangstates:Dong Hui, styledXiuxu, was fromXiangyang[襄陽]. When he enteredShu, he assistedFei Yiin being an envoy toWuasPalace Gentleman who Proclaims Faith. A greatly inebriatedSun Quanonce asked[Fei] Yi, “Yang YiandWei Yanare just shepherd servants and petty men! Though they only have contributed their flattery in the affairs of the time, you have nonetheless given them office, their power not to be trifled with. If you were deprived ofZhuge Liangone morning, they would cause chaos! You gentlemen are so doltish that you had never once considered that as a preemptive concern! How can this be said to be bequeathing arrangements to your descendants?”[Fei] Yiwas stunned and looked around. He was not able to give a reply.[Dong] Huieyed[Fei] Yiand said, “You should quickly say that even though the disharmony of[Yang] Yiand[Wei] Yanrose to a personal grievance, they lack the rowdy hearts ofQing [Bu]andHan [Xin]. At the present, we are moving to purge our strongfoeand unite the central lands. We would accomplish merits through their talent, and our undertaking would be expanded by their talent. If we give up this and do not give them office, then in fending off future threats, it would be like preparing for gusts and waves, yet rejecting and abandoning the boats and oars. That is not a lasting plan.”[Sun] Quanlaughed loudly in delight. WhenZhuge Liangheard this, he regarded them as words of discernment. Not even three days had gone by after the return [ofZhuge Liang] when he was recruited to be a staff member in the bureau of theAssisting Chancellor. He was promoted toGrand AdministratorofBa commandery[巴郡太守].89•••
Here, “sycophants” [鳴吠] literally means “crows and barks”.↩
These two are recorded as having plotted against theHan dynasty.↩
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would comment on this. TheChronicles of Han and Jinalso has this conversation, but it does not say that it wasDong Huiwho instructed thus. The speech itself also has a few differences. These two books both come fromMister Xi, yet are dissimilar in such a way. The base biography states, “This[Dong] Huiwas young in age and low in office”, but if he was already part of the bureau of theAssisting Chancellorbefore leaving to serve inBa commandery[巴郡], then his office would not be lowly. I thus suspect thatMister Xiis not meticulous with his words.
In the sixth year of theera [243], he becameGeneral Who Supports the State. In the seventh year [244], he became actingDirector of the SecretariatasPalace Attendant, and served as an assistant to theGrand General,Fei Yi.
In the ninth year [246], hedied.C
TheRecords of Huayang Statestates: At the time, the people of Shu [蜀] believed thatZhuge Liang,Jiang Wan,Fei Yi, and[Dong] Yunwere the four chancellors. It was one title with four prominent personages.
Chen Zhireplaced[Dong] YunasPalace Attendant, then colluded intimately withHuang Hao. This was when[Huang] Haobegan to control political affairs. After[Chen] Zhidied,[Huang] HaobecameRegular Palace AttendantfromDirector of the Yellow GatesandCavalry Commandant who Receives Chariots. He wielded and abused authority, which only ended when the state fell. There was no person inShuthat did not miss[Dong] Yun. WhenDeng Aiarrived inShu, he heard about the vices of[Huang] Hao, so he arrested and detained him. He was going to kill him, but[Huang] Haogave generous bribes to the attendants of[Deng] Ai, thus escaping.
***
[Chen] Zhi, styledFengzong, was fromRunan[汝南]. He was the maternal grandson of the older brother ofXu Jing. When he was young, he became fatherless and grew up in the household of[Xu] Jing. When he was capped, he had a famous reputation. He was promoted toGentleman of the Selection Bureau. He was dignified, strict, and had a dignified demeanor. He had many abilities and skills, and he possessed numerology and divination.10[Fei] Yisaw him as very extraordinary, thus he was promoted to succeed[Dong] Yunin palaceservice.•••
Here, “numerology and divination” [數術] refers to the many unorthodox techniques that were used to allegedly calculate fate.↩
WhenLü Yidied,[Chen] Zhialso became actingDirector of the SecretariatasPalace Attendantand also becameGeneral of the Garrison Army. AlthoughGrand GeneralJiang Weiwas higher in rank than[Chen] Zhi, he often led troops outside and rarely attended imperial governance.[Chen] Zhicatered to the will of hislordand he associated with eunuchs. He thus received deep trust and love, and his authority was greater than that of[Jiang] Wei.
In the first year of theera [258], he died. TheLatter Lordgrieved and mourned, crying tears when he spoke. He issued an edict, stating:
“Chen Zhimanaged duties for a long period of time, and being gentle and admirable, he was a paragon. He was competent and staid, being remarkable in such, and he was harmonious and righteous, being beneficial to everything. In his various accomplishments, he was truly wise. Alas, his life was not long and lasting! I feel so sorrowful! When the living have illustrious reputations, then upon death, they should be bestowed a flattering posthumous title. His posthumous title shall be theLoyalMarquis[忠侯].”
His son,[Chen] Can, was conferred asMarquis within the Passes, and his second son,[Chen] Yu, was selected to serve asAttending Gentleman at the Yellow Gates.
Ever since[Chen] Zhihad favor, theLatter Lordposthumously resented[Dong] Yun, which grew more deep with each day, and he even thought that he was belittled. The reason[Chen] Zhibecame the one man that was loved was because of the alienation and gradual discord [betweenLiu ShanandDong Yun] that was caused by[Huang] Hao.
***
The grandson of[Dong] Yun,[Dong] Hong, served as theGrand AdministratorofBaxi[巴西太守] underJin.D
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would have a thought. For[Chen] Tai, son ofChen Qun, and[Lu] Kang, son ofLu Xun, all of the biographies had these sons appended to their fathers, and did not separate their surnames in writing them out;11indeed, for the likes ofWang Su,Du Shu,Zhang Cheng, andGu Shao, there were none of them that were not so. It was onlyDong Yunwho was unlike them. I cannot fully discern the intention thereof. Might it be that the reputation and rank of[Dong] Yunwere very important and that his deeds surpassed those of his father?Xiahou XuanandChen Biaowere all praised for being red and horned,12but they also were like[Chen] Tai.13The Book of the Weicombined the name [ofXiahou Xuan] into the volume called theBiographies of the Various Xiahous and Caos, thus no more was added onto his evaluation.Chen Wuand[Chen] Biaoboth attained the position ofDeputy Generals, and their ranks did not surpass eachother.•••
Chen Shoudid not write the character for the surname when introducing the children of famed officials, for example, “[Lu] Kang” [抗] instead of “Lu Kang” [陸抗].Dong Yunis an exception, with this biography introducing him in full as “Dong Yun” [董允]. His surname is written separately despite him being the son of his father,Dong He; their blood relationship would be sufficient forChen Shounot to write down the Dong surname forDong Yunbecause the reader would already know that. This is probably whatPei Songzhimeans by “did not separate their surnames in writing them out” [不別載姓].↩
Here, “red and horned [calves]” [騂角] is a metaphor for wise and talented children of mediocre parents.↩
Xiahou XuanandChen Biaowere appended to the biographies of their fathers just like howChen Taiwas appended to the biography of his father, despitePei Songzhiimplying that they surpassed their fathers likeDong Yunhad.↩
